author: @NorthernKitty
text: I've seen a lot of fantastic comments with great advice on how to do this project better, which are no doubt vast improvements, but I'm glad you're sticking with the FEMA book as a baseline on this project.  Then perhaps implement those ideas later?  Most of us are learning this along with you, and I've always felt it's just as important on why NOT to do something by doing it the more basic and less effective way first, before moving on to a more advanced and better way.  Otherwise we might skip a "lesson learned" that gives us firsthand experience.  Basically, learning a technology via progression.  I used to do a lot of training in information technology, and I truly believe that approach works far better in preparing someone for problem-solving and troubleshooting later.  😊😊😊  "Shortcuts" get you to your destination faster, but you miss all the scenery along the way.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have always found it doesn't matter what you do - some one some where will tell you to do it differently - I have also found that two experts will give two quite often directly opposed instructions lol - basically I go with what I think lol

author: @scotttovey
text:
A good way to make a gasket for your lid:

1) Put put nuts on the threads and adjust them so that the lid and barrel have a 1/8th - 2/8th even gap all the way round.

2) Take the lid off and put a good 1/8th to 2/8th bead of sealer on the barrel.

3) Gently lay wax paper, wax side facing sealer all the way around the top of your barrel.

4) Set the lid on top and make sure you press it down so that the bars touch the nuts.

5) Let it cure over knight.

6) Once the sealant is cured, remove the lid and the wax paper. 

7) You should have a gasket that matches the bottom of your lid against which you can seal the lid.

Unless you have a better method of making a gasket like just cutting a large piece of rubber gasket.

I modified this from the Appliance Doctor; Steve Gannon's fix for worn refrigerator gaskets.

In that situation, you apply the sealant to the refrigerator's warn gasket, put the wax side of wax paper on it and close the door allowing it to cure overnight. When you remove the wax paper, the refrigerator will seal up tight.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice one mate - thanks for the tip

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering 😎👍

author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
text: Very innovative Robert!! You surely put your own twist on this unit :) Very cool design concept!!

replies.author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
replies.text: I would suggest using ceramic high temp seal on the reactor. Here in the States I use an adhesive backed seal that you stick in place and then bolt flange surfaces together. May look into that, I dont know if your sealer will hold up or not. I have never seen a sealant in a tube than can withstand more than 600* F and that is hardly high enough.

replies.author: @lokilyesmyth
replies.text: @Thrive-Off-Grid they have tube ones over here that are rated at 2200F   I’ve used them in hot places and they’ve held up. I don’t know what temp I got to, but it was more than 600.

replies.author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
replies.text: @lokilyesmyth Are you talking about the ceramic rope seal? Yes we also use a flanged version of that seal as well for our doors.

replies.author: @lokilyesmyth
replies.text: @Thrive-Off-Grid no they sell caulking tubes with sealant that comes out like silicone or acrylic caulking, but made for stoves and fireplaces

replies.author: @lokilyesmyth
replies.text: Look up ceresit CS 38
